one_line_takeaway: A position paper arguing 3DGS watermarking research should adopt an explicit adversary access-vector taxonomy and scenario-grounded threat models (rather than ad hoc robustness benchmarking), illustrated with a forensic-watermarking case study and a reproducible keyed spread-spectrum baseline in the native SH-parameter space showing the classical capacity-robustness-fidelity trade-off (PSNR from ~25dB down to ~59dB as embedding strength alpha drops from 0.1 to 0.001, and bit accuracy dropping as payload grows from 32 to 64 bits).
key_finding: The paper's central normative claim, illustrated rather than statistically proven, is that most existing 3DGS watermarking papers leave adversarial access unspecified, rely on public detectors (e.g., reused image detectors like HiDDeN) without justification, and omit explicit keying -- repeating mistakes already exposed in 1990s-2000s audio/image watermarking (notably the SDMI public-detector oracle-attack episode) -- so that reported robustness numbers do not translate into security guarantees once an adversary can query or backprop through the detector. Their own spread-spectrum baseline experiment demonstrates classical capacity-robustness-fidelity trade-offs concretely: at fixed payload B=32, PSNR/SSIM improve monotonically as embedding amplitude alpha decreases from 0.1 (PSNR 25.43, SSIM 0.825, LPIPS 0.255) to 0.01 (PSNR 44.96, SSIM 0.995, LPIPS 0.009) to 0.001 (PSNR 58.85, SSIM 0.998, LPIPS 0.001), with the same monotonic pattern holding at B=48 and B=64 (e.g. at alpha=0.001, PSNR 57.96/57.32 for B=48/64 respectively); fidelity degrades monotonically as payload increases at fixed alpha even when rendering differences are visually imperceptible. Under a fixed total embedding-energy budget and additive Gaussian noise, bit accuracy decreases monotonically as payload length increases from 32 to 64 bits across all three datasets (e.g. Blender: 0.867 at B=32 down to 0.703 at B=64; Mip-NeRF 360: 0.817 to 0.737; LLFF: 0.790 to roughly 0.717 across the shown points), confirming the classical capacity-robustness trade-off holds for this native-parameter-space keyed baseline.
core_contribution: This is a position/critique paper (not a new attack or defense algorithm) arguing that R&D on 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) watermarking is limited more by unclear security definitions than by embedding technique. Its own contributions are: (1) an 'access vector' formalism A = [Access-M, Access-Mw, Access-E, Access-D, Oracle-D, Oracle-R, Key-K] used to define black/grey/white-box adversary regimes for 3DGS watermarking in a way that is explicit about model access, detector access, oracle access, and key possession; (2) a scenario-driven analysis of two deployment settings -- forensic/fingerprint watermarking (main text) and copyright ownership verification (Appendix A) -- each decomposed into concrete sub-scenarios (e.g., cloud restreaming, passive leakage, tracing-portal probing, offline forensic tracing, oracle-guided model evasion, local/online white-box optimization) with an access vector and recommended keying mechanism per sub-scenario, drawn from lessons in classical audio/image/video watermarking (Cox et al. 1997, SDMI); (3) a descriptive reference table (Table 1, Table 2) surveying six existing 3DGS watermarking papers (GuardSplat, 3D-GSW, GS-Marker, MarkSplatter, Water-GS, GaussianMarker) along embedding domain/stage, detector privacy, datasets, and simulated threat models -- this is explicitly Related Work being organized, not the authors' own method; (4) a 'call to action' proposing a minimum-disclosure standard (state scenario+objective, state threat model via access vector, align algorithm design with threat model, report both effectiveness and exposure); and (5) an original, small-scale reproducible baseline: classical Cox et al. spread-spectrum (code-division multiplexing) watermarking applied directly in the native 3DGS spherical-harmonics parameter space (1D DCT over concatenated per-Gaussian SH coefficients, mid-band carrier selection, three separate HMAC-PRF-derived keys for payload mapping/carrier permutation/spreading codes, non-blind correlation detection), evaluated only on their own scheme under varying embedding amplitude and payload length -- not against the six surveyed methods.
threat_model: The paper formalizes an authenticated watermarking system (setup / embedding / verification) with a 7-entry adversary access vector A = (Access-M, Access-Mw, Access-E, Access-D, Oracle-D, Oracle-R, Key-K), where 'Access' = full access to an artifact (original model M, watermarked model Mw, embedder E, detector D) and 'Oracle' = query-only access via the verification portal (Oracle-D to the detector, Oracle-R to the renderer); Key-K=1 means the adversary knows the secret key. Black-box: no direct artifact access, only oracle queries (three sub-cases: cloud restreaming/screen-recording with no oracle access, passive leakage of fixed views, and iterative tracing-portal probing). White-box: full access to Mw plus embedder E and detector D internals/weights, key still unknown; local white-box optimization (gradient-based erasure with minimal distortion, or false framing) vs. online white-box optimization (also queries the portal). Grey-box: intermediate access, typically Access-Mw=1 but Access-D=0 (adversary can edit the model but not backprop through the detector), covering offline forensic tracing, oracle-guided model evasion, and offline/online piracy with model resale. Attacks are additionally grouped by level: 3DGS-model-level (parameter edits, pruning, resampling), image/video-level (compression, cropping, re-encoding, screen capture), and neural-network-level (gradient-based removal/spoofing against a queryable or leaked detector). Two use-case scenarios instantiate this framework: forensic/fingerprint watermarking (main text, Section 3) and copyright ownership verification (Appendix A).
limitations: The paper's own baseline is evaluated only against itself (varying alpha and B) on three datasets, not benchmarked head-to-head against any of the six neural 3DGS watermarking methods it surveys, so no claim is made that the spread-spectrum baseline is competitive in robustness/capacity with learned methods -- it is offered purely as a reproducible, fully key-explicit reference point. The evaluation robustness sweep uses a single attack family (additive Gaussian noise), not the fuller attack taxonomy (rotation, scaling, cropping, JPEG, dropout, etc.) the paper itself catalogs in Table 2 for other methods; no white/grey/black-box adversarial evasion experiments (e.g. gradient-based removal against their own detector) are run to validate the access-vector taxonomy empirically. As a position paper, the access-vector/box-regime taxonomy itself is not validated against any ground truth or independently verified beyond the authors' own analytical argument and analogy to legacy audio-visual watermarking practice.

quantitative_results: Figure 3: bit accuracy vs. payload B in {32,48,64} under additive Gaussian noise (sigma=0.3) and fixed total embedding energy -- Blender: 0.867 -> 0.833 -> 0.703 (approx, per plotted points 0.867/0.833/0.703 order uncertain across B), Mip-NeRF 360 and LLFF show similarly decreasing trends (values shown in figure: 0.817, 0.756, 0.737, 0.867, 0.833, 0.801, 0.790, 0.717, 0.703 across the three datasets and three payload sizes)
baselines_compared: None experimentally -- the paper's spread-spectrum baseline is evaluated only against itself across embedding amplitude and payload settings; Tables 1-2 summarize six other 3DGS watermarking papers (GuardSplat, 3D-GSW, GS-Marker, MarkSplatter, Water-GS, GaussianMarker) descriptively from their own published setups, not as re-run experimental baselines
